content.full_text:
i

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 6, 1992

Jayne F. Herman
Deputy County Counsel
1195 Third Street, Room 301
Napa, CA 94559-3001

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-556

Dear Ms. Herman:

This letter is in response to your request for advice and
confirms the advice I provided to you during the course of our
telephone conversation on December 20, 1991, regarding the duties
and responsibilities of Daniel M. Jonas under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Your request is general in nature and based on the information you
have provided we are unable to determine whether the effect of the
pending decisions on Mr. Jonas' economic interests will be
material. Accordingly, we are providing you with informal
assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.²

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Commissioner Daniel M. Jonas to disqualify himself from
participating in decisions regarding an inclusionary housing
ordinance which would encourage the provision of affordable
housing in connection with the development of residential
projects?

_______________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 2


CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Commissioner Jonas to disqualify himself from participating in
decisions regarding the proposed ordinance if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the pending governmental decisions will have a
material financial effect on his economic interests,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, as
discussed below.

FACTS

Daniel M. Jonas is a commissioner on the Napa County
Conservation, Development and Planning Commission. Commissioner
Jonas is president of, and holds one-third of the equity in,
Triple Delta Incorporated ("Triple Delta"), a closely-held
corporation located in the City of Napa, in Napa County. Triple
Delta regularly engages in the construction of residential and
commercial projects, both contractually and speculatively.
Neither Triple Delta nor Commissioner Jonas has owned or developed
any land in the unincorporated areas of Napa County during the
past twelve months nor does either, in the foreseeable future,
plan to purchase or develop any such land. Their future plans,
however, could change should either be presented with a favorable
development opportunity.

Commissioner Jonas' spouse, Janet E. Jonas, is currently
employed as a real estate agent for Coldwell Banker, Classical
Properties ("Coldwell Banker"), a real estate firm located in the
City of Napa, in Napa County. As a commissioned agent for
Coldwell Banker, Ms. Jonas assists the firm in marketing its
listed properties including, from time to time, existing homes
located in unincorporated areas of Napa County. Ms. Jonas
currently has one single-family home in escrow in an
unincorporated area of Napa County, but has earned no commissions
in the past twelve months from the sale of property located in
that area.

The housing element of the Napa County general plan states
that the provision of affordable housing in the county is to be
encouraged in connection with the development of residential
projects. The Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning
Commission is currently scheduled to consider whether to recommend
to the Napa County Board of Supervisors that an inclusionary
housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part of a
comprehensive response to this aspect of the county's general
plan. Such a recommendation, if made, could include enactment
of a model ordinance requiring some or all of the following:
(1)
that a certain percentage of all new residential units constructed
in a project be "affordable" as that term is defined in the
ordinance; (2) that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund
in lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 3


(3) that construction of a certain number of affordable units take
place off-site. In addition, the Napa County Conservation,
Development and Planning Commission is likely to recommend the
creation of additional sources of funding for affordable housing
through means such as assessment of linkage fees from developers
of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon
project-generated employment, imposition of a business license
tax, or increase in documentary transfer or sales taxes. Such
taxes, if imposed or increased, would be inapplicable to
Commissioner Jonas' business and property interest located within
the City of Napa. Should Commissioner Jonas or Triple Delta ever
decide to commence a development project in unincorporated areas
of Napa County, such a project would be subject to any ordinance
ultimately adopted by the Napa County Board of Supervisors.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest. Commissioner Jonas is a public
official. (Section 82048.)

Financial Interests

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a
member of the official's immediate family,3 or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.


_________________________
3 An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 4


(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103.

Commissioner Jonas has a number of potentially disqualifying
economic interests which must be evaluated to determine whether he
must disqualify himself from participating in governmental
decisions regarding the proposed ordinance. He has an economic
interest in Triple Deltas as president and holder of one third of
the equity of this closely-held corporation engaged in the
construction of residential and commercial projects. Commissioner
Jonas' economic interests include a pro-rata share of the income
of Triple Delta. (Section 82030.) In addition, clients of Triple
Delta are also considered sources of income to Commissioner Jonas.
(Russell Advice Letter, No. A-88-484.)

Mr. Jonas' economic interests also include his community
property interest in the income of his spouse. (Section 82030.)
You have advised us that Ms. Jonas is a real estate agent engaged
in the sale of real estate within the jurisdiction of Napa County.
Regulation 18704.3 provides that the following are deemed to be
sources of income to a real estate agent within the meaning of
subdivision (c) of Section 87103:

(A) The broker and brokerage business entity
under whose auspices the agent works;

(B) The person the agent represents in the
transaction; and

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 5


     (C)  Any person who receives a finder's or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.

                    Regulation 18704.3(c)(3).

     Accordingly, because Commissioner Jonas has a community
property interest in the income of his spouse, we must consider
the material effect of the pending decisions on the following
economic interests of his spouse: (1) Coldwell Banker which is
the broker and brokerage business where Ms. Jonas works as a
commissioned real estate agent; (2) Any person who has been a
source of commission income to Ms. Jonas of $500 or more in the
preceding 12 months; and (3) Any person who has received a fee for
referring a party to a transaction to Coldwell Banker.
Consequently, Commissioner Jonas must consider the foreseeable and
material financial effect of decisions regarding the proposed
ordinance on each of these economic interests to determine whether
he must disqualify himself from participating in such decisions.

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

     A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency.  (Regulation 18700(b).)  Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

     (1)  Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

     (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

          (A)  Conducting research or making any
     investigation which requires the exercise of
     judgment on the part of the official or
     designated employee and the purpose of which
     is to influence the decision; or

          (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
     analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
     which requires the exercise of judgment on the

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 6


part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
economic interests of Commissioner Jonas will be materially
affected by a governmental decision, he must not only disqualify
himself from participating in formal decisions of the Napa County
Conservation, Development and Planning Commission which may affect
such interests, but he must also abstain from attempting to
influence such decisions by communicating with other members of
the commission or the staff regarding the decisions.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. Downey v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
FPPC Ops. 198.). The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

During the course of our telephone conversation of
December 26, 1991, Mr. Jonas directed my attention to In re
Thorner, supra, where the Commission discussed the concept of
foreseeability within several hypothetical situations. In
Thorner, the Commission determined foreseeability within the
context of applicants in which a public official had a financial
interest appearing before the official's agency. The facts in
your request for advice do not involve applicants appearing before
the agency.

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 7


     Thorner, however, also discusses disqualification in a
context which is similar to the facts in your request for advice
and concludes that it is foreseeable that the financial interests
of the public official will be materially affected by the pending
decisions in a manner which is distinguishable from the effect on
the decisions on the public generally. In Thorner, the pending
decision related to lifting a building moratorium. The Commission
concluded that although the proposed decisions might result in
financial benefit to a large segment of the community, the public
official's business was in a position to realize immediate,
substantial and specific financial gains as a result of renewed
building activity if the building moratorium were lifted.
Accordingly, the effect of the pending decisions on the public
official were distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally and the official was disqualified from participating in
the decisions. (Thorner, supra, at p. 208.)

     Similarly, it is foreseeable that the proposed ordinance will
have an effect on Commissioner Jonas' economic interests. The
ordinance pertains to the development of affordable housing in the
County of Napa where both Triple Delta and Commissioner Jonas'
spouse do business. Requiring that developers either include
affordable housing in their projects or that they pay fees into a
fund to provide affordable housing is likely to affect the profits
for Triple Delta, a business entity engaged in the construction of
residential and commercial property. Also, as the cost of
developing residential and commercial property in the
unincorporated areas of the county increases, there is substantial
likelihood that commercial and residential development in the city
will be affected.

     It is also foreseeable that Mrs. Jonas' source of income,
Coldwell Banker, will be affected by the proposed ordinance.
Affordable housing prices tend to be lower thus generating lower
sales commissions for the real estate agents who represent
buyers and sellers in transactions involving such homes.
Moreover, Mrs. Jonas' commission income will be affected similarly
by the proposed ordinance. On the other hand, more affordable
housing may result in increased sales and, consequently, increased
commissions. Accordingly, the foreseeability element is met.4 We
now determine whether the effect of the pending decisions on
Commissioner Jonas' economic interests will be material.


4     During the course of our telephone conversation of December 26,
1991, Commissioner Jonas advised me that Triple Delta had not done
any business in the unincorporated areas of the county during the
last five years. To the extent that Commissioner Jonas believes
that it is not foreseeable that the proposed ordinance will have
an effect on his economic interests, we defer to his judgment.

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 8


Materiality

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is
materially affected by a decision. If the official's economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
However, when the official's economic interests will be affected
indirectly by a decision, the appropriate standards for
determining materiality are those of Regulations 18702.2 through
18702.6. These regulations are discussed below.

A. Economic Interests Directly Involved in the Decision

A person or business entity is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person or entity,
either personally or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

Regulation 18702.1(b).

It does not appear that any of Commissioner Jonas' economic
interests will be directly involved when the commission considers
the proposed ordinance and affordable housing issues. Therefore,
the appropriate standards for determining materiality are those
discussed below.

B. Economic Interests Indirectly Involved in the Decision

1. Business Interests

a. Triple Delta

Commissioner Jonas is president and holds one third of the
equity of Triple Delta, a closely-held corporation engaged in the
construction of residential and commercial projects. For purposes

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 9


of our analysis we assume that Commissioner Jonas has an
investment of at least $1,000 in this business. Presumably, this
business has been a source of income to Commissioner Jonas of $250
or more in the preceding 12 months. It is reasonably foreseeable
that this business interest will be affected by decisions
regarding the proposed ordinance as the business may, in the
future, engage in construction which will be subject to the terms
of the proposed ordinance.

This business entity will be affected indirectly by decisions
regarding the proposed ordinance. When an official has an
economic interest in a business entity indirectly involved in the
decision, the appropriate standard for determining materiality is
that of Regulation 18702.2, for a business such as Triple Delta,
the applicable standards are those of Subdivision (g) of
Regulation 18702.2 which states that the effect of a decision upon
a business entity indirectly involved in a decision is material
if:

     (1)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2)  The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g).

If a decision will affect a business entity in which an
official has an economic interest in the amounts set forth above,
the effect of the decision is material. We have insufficient
information to make this determination. Commissioner Jonas must
assess the potential effect of decisions regarding the proposed
requirements for affordable housing or in lieu fees upon this
business interest. Among the factors to be considered in making
this evaluation is whether the decisions will result in increased
costs to this business as the requirements of the proposed
ordinance may impose certain additional expenses on builders of
residential and commercial property. Or, if Triple Delta does not
develop any property in the unincorporated areas of the county,
the proposed ordinance may result in increased business in the
incorporated areas of the county because of the increased cost of
doing business in the unincorporated areas. If Commissioner Jonas
determines that the effect of such decisions will be material, he
must disqualify himself from participating in such decisions.

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 10


b.  Coldwell Banker

     Section 87103(c) states that an official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on any source of income aggregating $250 or more in the
twelve months preceding a governmental decision. Commissioner
Jonas has a community property interest in the income of his
spouse who is employed by Coldwell Banker. If Ms. Jonas has
received $500 or more from Coldwell Banker in the preceding twelve
months, Mr. Jonas may not participate in governmental decisions
which will have a foreseeable material effect on Coldwell Banker.
The appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.2(g) discussed above.

     2.  Clients of Triple Delta

     Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual
includes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater. Consequently
any person or business that has made a payment to Triple Delta is
a source of income to Commissioner Jonas if his pro-rata share of
the payment is $250 or greater. (Kolodney Advice Letter, No. I-
91-462.) Therefore, Commissioner Jonas may not participate in any
decision which will reasonably foreseeably have a material
financial effect on that person or business, as discussed below.

     3.  Sources of Commission Income

     Commissioner Jonas' spouse has received commission income
from persons she has represented in real estate transactions. One
half of any such commission income is Commissioner Jonas'
community property interest. Accordingly, any person who has
been a source of commission income to Ms. Jonas of $500 or more in
the preceding twelve months is potentially a disqualifying
financial interest.

     In addition, Ms. Jonas is acting as an agent for the sale or
purchase of real property in the unincorporated areas of the
county. Thus, she is the recipient of "promised" income within
the meaning of subdivision (c) of Section 87103. This is so
because commission income is deemed "promised" income to a real



5  "Person" means an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company,
corporation, association, committee, and any other organization or
group of persons acting in concert. (Section 82047.)

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 11


estate agent when the sale is pending (i.e., the sale is in escrow).  (Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-82-192; Armento Advice
Letter, No. I-90-032.)

     If any of the sources of income or promised income mentioned
above will be materially affected by decisions regarding the
proposed ordinance, Commissioner Jonas must disqualify himself
from participating in such decisions.  The standard for
determining materiality with regard to an individual who is a
source of income of $250 or more to a public official in the
twelve months preceding a decision and who is indirectly involved
in a governmental decision is that of Regulation 18702.6 which
states that:

          The effect of a decision is material as to an
          individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
          official if any of the following applies:

               (a)  The decision will affect the individual's
          income, investments, or other tangible or
          intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
          property) by $1,000 or more; or

               (b)  The decision will affect the individual's
          real property interest in a manner that is
          considered material under Section 18702.3 or
          Section 18702.4.

                              Regulation 18702.6.

     Please note, however, that if the source of income is a
business entity indirectly involved in the decision, then the
applicable standards for determining materiality are those of
Regulation 18702.2 discussed above with reference to Triple Delta.

Public Generally

     Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision is material, disqualification is required
only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally.  (Section 87100.)  If the decision does not
affect all members of the public in the same manner,
disqualification may be required unless the effect of the decision
on the public official's economic interests is the same as the
effect on a significant segment of the public.  (Regulation
18703.)  For purposes of our discussion, the public is the entire
population of the county.  (In re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In
re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77; Jorgensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-
017.)

_____________________

     6  Enclosed for your reference are copies of Regulations 18702.3
and 18702.4.

Our File No. I-91-556
Page 12


     We conclude that the "public generally" exception is
inapplicable to Commissioner Jonas.  This is so because his
economic interests are peculiarly tied in with real estate
construction and sales, both areas which will be singularly
affected by the terms of the proposed ordinance seeking to provide
affordable housing in Napa County.

     We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.7

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Blanca M. Breeze
                                            Counsel, Legal Division
SH:BMB:bb

Enclosures:  Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.4.




_____________________________

7  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

QUESTION: Do the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act require
Commi.ssioner DanieI M. Jonas to disqualify himself fron
participating in decisions regardinq an inclusionary housing
ordinance which would encourage the provision of affordable
housing in connection with the development of residential
proj ects?

Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 322.5660


our Page 2

CONCLUSION: The conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act require

embedding.first_500_words: i California Fair Political Practices Commission January 6, 1992 Jayne F. Herman Deputy County Counsel 1195 Third Street, Room 301 Napa, CA 94559-3001 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-91-556 Dear Ms. Herman: This letter is in response to your request for advice and confirms the advice I provided to you during the course of our telephone conversation on December 20, 1991, regarding the duties and responsibilities of Daniel M. Jonas under the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your request is general in nature and based on the information you have provided we are unable to determine whether the effect of the pending decisions on Mr. Jonas' economic interests will be material. Accordingly, we are providing you with informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.² QUESTION Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Commissioner Daniel M. Jonas to disqualify himself from participating in decisions regarding an inclusionary housing ordinance which would encourage the provision of affordable housing in connection with the development of residential projects? _______________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-91-556 Page 2 CONCLUSION The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Commissioner Jonas to disqualify himself from participating in decisions regarding the proposed ordinance if it is reasonably foreseeable that the pending governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on his economic interests, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, as discussed below. FACTS Daniel M. Jonas is a commissioner on the Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission. Commissioner Jonas is president of, and holds one-third of the equity in, Triple Delta Incorporated ("Triple Delta"), a closely-held corporation located in the City of Napa, in Napa County. Triple Delta regularly engages in the construction of residential and commercial projects, both contractually and speculatively. Neither Triple Delta nor Commissioner Jonas has owned or developed any land in the unincorporated areas of Napa County during the past twelve months nor does either, in the foreseeable future, plan to purchase or develop any such land. Their future plans, however, could change should either be presented with a favorable development opportunity. Commissioner Jonas' spouse, Janet E. Jonas, is currently employed as a real estate agent for Coldwell Banker, Classical Properties ("Coldwell Banker"), a real estate firm located in the City of Napa, in Napa County. As a commissioned agent for Coldwell Banker, Ms. Jonas assists the firm in marketing its listed properties including, from time to time, existing homes located in unincorporated areas of Napa County. Ms. Jonas
